data.title: ChatGPT Prompts for LinkedIn Thought Leaders
data.metaTitle: 15 ChatGPT Prompts for LinkedIn Thought Leaders (Grow Your Audience) 2026
data.metaDescription: ChatGPT prompts for LinkedIn thought leaders to write viral posts, engage followers, build authority, and convert connections into clients or opportunities.
data.keywords: chatgpt prompts for linkedin, linkedin thought leadership prompts, ai prompts linkedin posts, linkedin content strategy chatgpt
data.heading: 15 ChatGPT Prompts for LinkedIn That Grow Your Audience and Influence
data.intro: LinkedIn rewards consistent, specific, and opinionated content. These prompts give you the formats that generate the most reach, engagement, and business impact — whether you're building a following from scratch or scaling an existing one.

prompts.title: Contrarian opinion post (gets engagement)
prompts.prompt: Write a LinkedIn post sharing a contrarian opinion about [COMMON BELIEF IN MY INDUSTRY]. Structure: 1-line hook that challenges the belief, 3 short paragraphs building the argument with real examples, and a final reframe of the better approach. Under 1,300 characters. End with a question to spark comments.
prompts.tip: Contrarian posts generate 3x more comments than agreement posts. The algorithm rewards engagement, not approval.

prompts.title: Personal story post (vulnerability + lesson)
prompts.prompt: Write a LinkedIn post sharing a professional lesson learned from a personal failure or challenge: [BRIEF CONTEXT OF CHALLENGE]. Structure: the situation (2-3 lines), what I did wrong or misunderstood, the turning point, the lesson I took away, and how I apply it now. Under 1,300 characters. Raw and honest, not self-deprecating.
prompts.tip: Vulnerability posts on LinkedIn generate the highest save-rate of any format — people save them to share later or revisit.

prompts.title: Tactical list post (high save rate)
prompts.prompt: Write a LinkedIn list post with [X] things [AUDIENCE] needs to know about [TOPIC]. Format: intro line that teases the value, numbered list (each item is 1-3 lines with a bold statement + brief explanation), and a closing line with a question or call to share. Under 1,500 characters. Actionable, specific, no filler.
prompts.tip: List posts are the most-saved format on LinkedIn. Each item should be specific enough to screenshot and use immediately.

prompts.title: Industry insight post (establishes authority)
prompts.prompt: Write a LinkedIn post sharing an insight about [INDUSTRY TREND OR DATA POINT]. Structure: stat or observation as a hook, 2-3 paragraphs of your unique interpretation (not just repeating the data), and a clear 'what this means for [AUDIENCE]' section. Under 1,300 characters. Cite your source in the first line.
prompts.tip: Insight posts that interpret data (not just share it) position you as an analyst, not just a commentator.

prompts.title: Newsletter promotion post (grow subscribers)
prompts.prompt: Write a LinkedIn post promoting my newsletter '[NEWSLETTER NAME]' on [TOPIC]. Tease the content of the latest issue (3 specific insights or frameworks, no spoilers). Explain who it's for and why it's worth subscribing. CTA: link in comments or bio. Under 1,200 characters. Feels like value, not a pitch.
prompts.tip: Newsletter promo posts that reveal 2-3 specific insights (without giving them all away) convert followers to subscribers 4x better than generic 'subscribe here' posts.

prompts.title: Hiring post (attract top talent)
prompts.prompt: Write a LinkedIn hiring post for a [ROLE] position at [COMPANY]. Lead with the mission and impact of the role (not the job title). Describe what great looks like (not just requirements). Explain what's unique about your team or culture. Include a direct apply link or CTA to DM. Under 800 characters. Attract, don't screen.
prompts.tip: Hiring posts that describe the problem the role will solve attract 2x better candidates than those that lead with job requirements.

prompts.title: Comment reply strategy (engage top posts)
prompts.prompt: Write 5 thoughtful comment templates for engaging on LinkedIn posts in [MY NICHE]. Each comment should: add a new perspective or data point, be 2-4 sentences long, reference something specific from the original post, and end with a question or insight that adds to the conversation. Not generic praise. Real engagement.
prompts.tip: Commenting on top posts in your niche drives more profile views than most original posts. High-quality comments get noticed.

prompts.title: Event/webinar announcement post
prompts.prompt: Write a LinkedIn post announcing [EVENT NAME] on [DATE]. Cover: who it's for (specific audience), what they'll learn (3 specific outcomes), format and duration, and a CTA to register via [LINK]. Create genuine urgency (limited seats or live Q&A). Under 1,000 characters.
prompts.tip: Event announcement posts with 3 specific learning outcomes get 3x more registrations than 'join us for...' posts.

prompts.title: Creator behind-the-scenes post
prompts.prompt: Write a LinkedIn post sharing behind-the-scenes of [A PROJECT/CONTENT/BUSINESS INITIATIVE I'M WORKING ON]. Include: what you're building, why, one challenge you've run into, and one thing you've learned. Invite others to ask questions or share similar experiences. Under 1,200 characters. Authentic process content.
prompts.tip: Behind-the-scenes content builds parasocial connection — followers feel invested in your journey and are more likely to support your work.

prompts.title: Polarizing question post (drive comments)
prompts.prompt: Write a LinkedIn post posing a polarizing question about [TOPIC] that has no obvious right answer. Frame it as a genuine debate worth having. Briefly share your position in 2-3 lines, then invite the audience to weigh in. Under 800 characters. Designed to generate comments, not likes.
prompts.tip: Debate posts that show the poster's own position (not just neutral questions) generate 50% more comments.

prompts.title: Reshare + commentary post
prompts.prompt: I want to reshare [ARTICLE/POST/STAT ABOUT TOPIC] with my own perspective. Write the commentary text for the reshare (100-150 characters for the post text). My angle: [MY OPINION/INTERPRETATION]. Should add genuine value to what the original source said — not just 'great read'.
prompts.tip: Reshares with substantive commentary (not 'great post!') are treated as original content by the LinkedIn algorithm.

prompts.title: Monthly review / reflection post
prompts.prompt: Write a LinkedIn post reflecting on [MONTH] at [COMPANY/PROJECT]. Include: 1 major win, 1 lesson from a failure or setback, 1 thing I'm looking forward to next month. Specific, honest, and brief — 3 short sections, under 1,000 characters total. Show the real work, not a highlight reel.
prompts.tip: Monthly review posts build a documented public journey — followers become invested in your progress over time.

prompts.title: Post announcing a new role or milestone
prompts.prompt: Write a LinkedIn post announcing [NEW JOB/PROMOTION/BUSINESS MILESTONE] for [NAME]. Include: the news (1 line), what this role/milestone means personally, a thank-you to 2-3 key people (without being sycophantic), and what I'm focused on next. Under 1,000 characters. Gracious, excited, not self-congratulatory.
prompts.tip: Milestone posts that thank specific people and look forward (not just backward) resonate more than pure self-celebration.

prompts.title: Outreach message (to new connection)
prompts.prompt: Write a LinkedIn connection request message to [PROSPECT/COLLABORATOR NAME] who is a [TITLE] at [COMPANY]. Reference a specific piece of their content or work ([DETAIL]). Mention a genuine reason for connecting (not a pitch). Under 200 characters (connection request limit).
prompts.tip: Never pitch in the connection request. The goal is the connection — the conversation comes after.

prompts.title: Viral hook rewrite (improve your existing post)
prompts.prompt: Here is my LinkedIn post draft: [PASTE YOUR DRAFT]. Rewrite the first 2 lines as 5 alternative hook options that are more likely to stop the scroll. Keep the body of the post the same. Hook types to try: curiosity gap, bold claim, surprising stat, relatable pain point, and counterintuitive statement.
prompts.tip: The first 2 lines are the only thing 80% of people read before deciding to click 'see more'. Everything else is secondary.
